However, despite the existing euro stablecoin ecosystem, its size remains a fraction of USDT's global standing. The political winds are shifting towards "sovereign" territories. The French finance minister has called for an increase in euro-pegged stablecoins and welcomed the launch of a joint euro stablecoin by ING, UniCredit, and BNP Paribas in the second half of 2026. MiCA has yet to answer the question: Can Europe create a euro alternative that people are willing to use sustainably? Who truly benefits from MiCA? Coincub's 2025 Crypto Asset Risk Report ranks all EU countries 22nd in regulatory rankings. MiCA has created a compliance bottleneck. The compliance cost floor (legal and setup fees of €50,000-€100,000, plus capital requirements, compliance personnel, and banking relationships) is viable for a Cayman entity backed by $500 million in venture capital, but absolutely unfeasible for pre-seed startups in Europe. Of the top 100 exchanges by global traffic, only eight are EU-native brands. Bitpanda is the only EU-native exchange in the upper half of the board. The compliance jobs created by banks like DekaBank, DZ Bank, and Commerzbank are real, but they are not innovative. Startups that should have hired engineers have either moved to Dubai, gone offshore and relied on reverse solicitation, or gone out of business. Europe is losing blockchain graduates. MiCA: More Than One License For EMT-intensive businesses, one license is never enough. The European Banking Authority (EBA) intervened twice on the PSD2 overlap issue (June 2025 and February 2026) because CASPs trading electronic currency tokens could also fall into the payment services sector. Prior to the second opinion, more than 100 CASPs had contacted national authorities. These companies' story was: "Get MiCA first, then deal with PSD2." The same names have consistently won because they have group structures capable of absorbing the costs of dual compliance, which eliminates smaller players. The impending breaking point: July 1st. The "grandfather clause" transition period will end on July 1, 2026. At that time, all entities not authorized by MiCA must cease operations. Poland has no way to proceed; approvals in Estonia are still pending; and the regulatory designation in Romania remains "pending publication." The peak of 45 licenses issued in December 2025 demonstrates the backlog clearing driven by deadlines. This pattern is about to repeat itself, and this time without extensions. From preparation to submission takes 9-18 months, and for those still in the transition phase and haven't submitted their applications, the window is effectively closed. ESMA Takes Over Everything The European Commission's Market Integration Plan, released in December 2025, proposes transferring all direct oversight of CASPs from national authorities to the ESMA in Paris, meaning unified authorization, monitoring, and enforcement. The European Central Bank (ECB) formally endorsed this move on April 9, 2026, spearheaded by France, Italy, and Austria. Countries like Malta are strongly resisting, as they have built competitive advantages under the "centralized model," which are now being told these advantages will be eliminated. The "license leasing" and "white label" models built on a loose, centralized foundation are directly threatened. While the ECB's opinion is not binding and negotiations will continue for months, the decentralized regulatory model that launched MiCA is already crumbling before the first version is even finalized. Institutional Signals Last week, Deutsche Bank acquired a 1.5% stake in Kraken for $200 million, valuing the company at $13.3 billion. Kraken confirmed on the same day that it had filed a confidential listing application with the SEC. The partnership covers trading, custody, settlement, and tokenized assets. The "accelerated rather than stagnant" institutional adoption story we described in our 2025 forecast review found its clearest expression in Frankfurt. The convergence of traditional finance and cryptocurrency is underway, but a European-based exchange has yet to capture this wave. Digital Euro Our 2025 report mentioned a launch of the digital euro in October 2025. This is inaccurate, as there was never a mandatory launch target in 2025. In October 2025, the ECB ended the preparatory phase and moved to the next phase. If legislation is passed in 2026, pilot programs could begin in mid-2027. The realistic launch date is 2029. The project is still progressing, slower than the ECB anticipated, and is increasingly framed as a “sovereign response” to US card organizations and Chinese payment platforms. What MiCA is and is not MiCA has fulfilled its mission, and many critics owe ESMA an apology. MiCA provided legal certainty, consumer protection standards, a public accountability registry, no expiration dates, a pass structure, and service code segmentation—truly excellent regulatory design. However, the unintended consequence is a single crypto market increasingly served by US companies, Asian entities with Cayman Islands email addresses, and German retail banks that treat crypto as a compliance checkbox, rather than by the European startups nominally intended to be nurtured by the Digital Finance Strategy. Therefore, from one perspective, MiCA is a victory for the regulatory architecture; but whether it represents a success for economic policy may largely depend on how things unfold in the coming months. In conclusion, the story of European crypto regulation is entering its most impactful chapter.

data.title: Yang Haipo: A Brief Discussion on the Base Interest Rate in Perpetual Contract Funding Rates
data.description:
Author: Yang Haipo, Founder and CEO of ViaBTC & CoinEx; Source: X, @yhaiyang

The funding rate for perpetual contracts on cryptocurrency exchanges includes a base interest rate of approximately 0.01%. This rate is charged three times daily, equivalent to an annualized rate of approximately 10%. This means that even if there is no basis between the contract price and the spot price, the market will still charge a positive funding rate of 10% annualized for long positions.

This rate has two effects. On the one hand, it provides a base return of 5% to 10% annualized for funding rate arbitrageurs in the market. The reason it doesn't reach the full 10% is because arbitrage inherently involves leverage—you need to buy the spot market while simultaneously shorting the contract, resulting in a capital utilization rate that cannot reach 100%. Depending on the trader's leverage ratio and risk management preferences, the final annualized return typically falls between 5% and 10%. On the other hand, this fee is essentially a continuous "tax" for long positions. While the percentage charged per transaction may seem low, for frequent traders, the annual interest cost after adding leverage is actually very high, sometimes exceeding the principal itself. So, where does this figure come from? In reality, many people have never delved into the underlying reasons, and even the designers of most exchanges today, such as Binance and OKX, may not understand the original intention behind setting this fee rate. This is because, essentially, most products in the industry are copied from each other, and product managers don't understand why such a mechanism exists. Smaller exchanges, lacking autonomy, can only passively align with larger exchanges; and large exchanges initially imitated BitMEX. As for why BitMEX set this figure, we need to go back to the market structure at the time. Today, USDT is widely used as margin, while BitMEX used BTC as margin in 2017 and 2018. Under that mechanism, going long implied a need to borrow USD, and going short implied a need to borrow BTC. At that time, BitMEX mainly referenced Bitfinex's interest rate market. On Bitfinex, the annualized interest rate for borrowing USD was around 10%, while the interest rate for borrowing BTC was close to zero, because the market was dominated by long positions and short positions were relatively few. Therefore, BitMEX introduced this approximately 10% annualized base interest rate and dynamically adjusted it according to market conditions. Although the market structure and interest rate environment have changed dramatically since then, this basic funding rate has been copied and retained. To put it bluntly, everyone has long forgotten the original logic in the process of copying each other, and no one dares to change this core value. More importantly, under the current USDT standard, following the same logic, the "correct" base interest rate should equal the USDT lending rate minus the lending rate of the underlying asset. USDT's interest rate in mainstream lending markets is only three or four basis points, while assets like BTC and ETH have staking and on-chain native rewards, meaning their lending rates are not zero. Subtracting the two, the theoretical base interest rate should be far below 10%, and for some assets, it might even be negative. The problem isn't just the source of the value; under the new margin system, even the direction is wrong. Moreover, this erroneous parameter is itself locked in by arbitrage funds. Funding rates should be a dynamic, market-converged mechanism. An artificially preset non-zero base interest rate is equivalent to adding a fixed-direction bias—this is no longer a convergence mechanism, but a transfer payment disguised as an interest rate. The recipients are delta-neutral arbitrage funds: long spot + short futures, profiting from the difference between the base interest rate and the lending cost. This portion of funds accumulates and is layered on exchange rates, staking rewards, and USDe (a synthetic US dollar), making the base interest rate no longer a parameter but a constant priced into countless balance sheets. Ethena's sUSDE, various USD-based "financial management" products, and market makers' carry strategies all implicitly assume this 10% will continue to exist. Whoever changes it first is the disruptor: lowering it leads to arbitrage funds withdrawing, contract depth collapsing, market maker spreads widening, and users migrating directly to counterparties that don't change it. Therefore, the rational strategy is for no one to change it. However, objectively speaking, this figure does indeed lay the foundation for a basic annualized rate of return for the entire crypto market. Around this funding rate, not only has a huge amount of arbitrage funds and related asset management projects and businesses emerged, but token projects such as USDe have also been developed. These derivative innovations are essentially financial management methods built on this base interest rate. It is no longer just an exchange parameter, but the default "crypto dollar risk-free rate"—an artificial anchor solidified by plagiarism, fueled by arbitrage funds, and maintained through collusion among all participants.
